title: Microbiome and Immunity
subtitle: Gut-brain connection. Prebiotics, probiotics, fermented foods. How nutrition affects mood
description: This checklist is a deep dive into the gut microbiome and its influence on immunity, psyche, and overall health. You'll learn how trillions of bacteria living inside us manage inflammation, synthesize neurotransmitters, and modulate immune response. We'll break down the difference between prebiotics and probiotics, learn how to choose effective fermented foods, and build a diet supporting a healthy microbiota and resilient psyche.

items.title: The Microbiome as a Separate Organ
items.text: The human microbiome is the collection of microorganisms (bacteria, archaea, fungi, viruses) inhabiting the gut, skin, oral cavity, and other mucous membranes. In the large intestine alone live about 38 trillion bacteria — roughly the same number as our own cells. Their collective genome is 150 times larger than the human genome. The microbiome performs functions our body cannot perform on its own: breaks down indigestible dietary fiber, synthesizes vitamins (K, B12, biotin, folate), produces short-chain fatty acids (butyrate, acetate, propionate), trains the immune system, protects against pathogens, and communicates directly with the brain via the vagus nerve and neurotransmitters. Microbiome diversity is a key health indicator: the more different bacterial species, the more resilient the system.
items.tip: Think of the microbiome as a garden you cultivate. Diverse plant-based nutrition = diverse 'seeds' and 'fertilizers' for beneficial bacteria.

items.title: The Gut-Brain Axis: How Bacteria Affect Mood
items.text: The gut and brain are connected by a two-way communication system involving the vagus nerve, immune signals, hormones, and bacterial metabolites. 90% of serotonin — the 'happiness hormone' — is produced in the enterochromaffin cells of the gut, and bacteria directly influence this process. Certain strains (Lactobacillus, Bifidobacterium) synthesize GABA — the main inhibitory neurotransmitter, reducing anxiety. Others produce dopamine precursors. Dysbiosis (microbiome imbalance) is associated with depression, anxiety disorders, autism, and even Parkinson's disease. Studies show: transplanting microbiota from depressed mice to healthy ones induces depressive behavior, and vice versa.
items.tip: If you feel anxiety or depression, look at your diet over the last 2-3 weeks. Was there enough fiber and fermented food? The gut 'remembers' eating habits and responds with mood.

items.title: Prebiotics: Food for Your Bacteria
items.source: International Scientific Association for Probiotics and Prebiotics (ISAPP)
items.text: Prebiotics are indigestible dietary fibers that selectively stimulate the growth and activity of beneficial bacteria in the large intestine. Unlike probiotics (live bacteria), prebiotics are a substrate, 'fertilizer.' Main types of prebiotics: inulin, fructooligosaccharides (FOS), galactooligosaccharides (GOS), resistant starch. Bacteria ferment these fibers, producing short-chain fatty acids, especially butyrate — the main fuel for gut epithelial cells (colonocytes). Butyrate reduces inflammation, strengthens the intestinal barrier, and protects against 'leaky gut syndrome.' Daily prebiotic norm is 5-10 grams, but the average modern person gets only 1-3 grams.
items.tip: Best sources of prebiotics: chicory root (inulin), Jerusalem artichoke, onion, garlic, leek, asparagus, green bananas, oats (beta-glucans), cooled cooked potato and rice (resistant starch).

items.title: Probiotics: Live Bacteria and How to Choose Them
items.source: Mary Ellen Sanders — Probiotics Expert Consensus
items.text: Probiotics are live microorganisms that, when administered in adequate amounts, confer a health benefit. Key words: 'live', 'adequate amounts', 'proven benefit.' Not all yogurts and supplements work. For effectiveness, a probiotic must: 1) contain an identified strain (not just 'Lactobacillus', but 'Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG'), 2) have a sufficient number of CFU (colony-forming units) — usually 1 to 100 billion per dose, 3) be viable at the time of consumption (expiration date, proper storage). Important: probiotics from supplements rarely colonize the gut long-term — they work 'transiently,' modulating the immune response and displacing pathogens as they pass through the GI tract. For long-term colonization, prebiotics and fermented foods are needed.
items.tip: Look for supplements with proven strains: Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG (for diarrhea and allergies), Bifidobacterium longum BB536 (for anxiety and constipation), Saccharomyces boulardii (for dysbiosis after antibiotics).

items.title: Fermented Foods: Natural Probiotics
items.source: Sandor Katz — The Art of Fermentation
items.text: Fermentation is the oldest method of food preservation, where microorganisms (bacteria, yeasts, molds) convert sugars and starches into acids, alcohol, and gases. During the process, live probiotic cultures are formed, as well as new vitamins and bioactive peptides. Key difference from store-bought probiotics: fermented foods contain complex microbial consortia (tens or hundreds of strains), not 1-2 isolated strains. This is more consistent with the natural diversity of the microbiome. However, it's important to distinguish live from pasteurized fermented products. Barrel sauerkraut from the market contains live bacteria. Canned sauerkraut from the supermarket that has undergone heat treatment does not.
items.tip: Include 1-2 servings of live fermented food daily: kefir, unsweetened live-culture yogurt, unpasteurized sauerkraut, kimchi, miso paste, natto, home-brewed kombucha.

items.title: Microbiome and Immunity: 70% of the Immune System Lives in the Gut
items.text: Gut-associated lymphoid tissue (GALT) is the largest immune organ in the body. The intestinal mucosa is the boundary between the 'outside' world (gut contents) and the 'internal' environment. Here, constant 'training' of immune cells occurs: distinguishing harmless antigens (food, commensal bacteria) from dangerous ones (pathogens). The microbiome plays a key role in this process. Certain bacteria (Bacteroides fragilis, Clostridia clusters) stimulate the development of regulatory T-cells (Tregs), which suppress excessive inflammation and prevent autoimmune reactions. Dysbiosis reduces Treg production, leading to chronic low-grade inflammation — the basis of most modern diseases: from allergies and asthma to type 2 diabetes and atherosclerosis.
items.tip: Supporting the microbiome is prevention not only of intestinal infections but also of systemic inflammatory diseases. Each serving of vegetables with fiber is a contribution to 'educating' your immune system.

items.title: Antibiotics and the Microbiome: How to Recover After a Course
items.text: Antibiotics save lives, but they are a nuclear bomb for the microbiome. One course of broad-spectrum antibiotics can wipe out 30-50% of the species diversity of gut bacteria. While total numbers recover within 1-2 months, the species composition can remain depleted for years. Some species disappear forever. This is especially critical in childhood: early antibiotic use is associated with increased risk of obesity, allergies, asthma, and inflammatory bowel disease later in life. After a course of antibiotics, a targeted recovery strategy is necessary: prebiotics (food for survivors), probiotics (temporary support), fermented foods (restoring diversity), elimination of sugar and processed foods (feed for pathogens).
items.tip: After antibiotics: 1) Probiotic Saccharomyces boulardii during and for 2 weeks after the course (resistant to antibiotics, prevents C. diff), 2) Prebiotics (inulin, GOS) to feed surviving bacteria, 3) Diverse fermented foods for 2-3 months.

items.title: Sugar, Emulsifiers, and the 'Western Diet': How Nutrition Kills the Microbiome
items.source: Benoir Chassaing — Dietary Emulsifiers and Microbiota
items.text: The modern 'Western diet' — high in sugars, refined carbohydrates, saturated fats, low in fiber — is the main driver of dysbiosis. Excess sugar and simple carbohydrates feed not the beneficial bacteria in the large intestine, but pathogenic and opportunistic ones in the small intestine, contributing to SIBO (small intestinal bacterial overgrowth). Particularly dangerous are food emulsifiers (polysorbate-80, carboxymethylcellulose), added to ice cream, sauces, baked goods to create a creamy texture. Studies show: emulsifiers destroy the protective mucus layer of the intestine, causing direct inflammation and altering microbiome composition in favor of pro-inflammatory species.
items.tip: Read ingredient labels. Avoid products with emulsifiers (E433, E466), artificial sweeteners (aspartame, sucralose — they also negatively affect the microbiome), and added sugar in the first three ingredients.

items.title: Fiber and Polyphenols: A Double Blow Against Dysbiosis
items.text: Professor Tim Spector, head of the British Gut Project, highlights two key nutritional components for microbiome health: soluble fiber (prebiotics) and polyphenols. Polyphenols are plant compounds giving color, taste, and aroma to fruits, vegetables, tea, coffee, cocoa, olive oil. Only 5-10% of polyphenols are absorbed in the small intestine. The remaining 90-95% reach the large intestine, where they serve as food for specific bacteria (e.g., Akkermansia muciniphila, associated with leanness and healthy metabolism). Bacteria break down polyphenols into smaller bioactive molecules, which are then absorbed and exert systemic anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects.
items.tip: Aim for a 'rainbow on your plate' and plant food diversity. Target 30 different plant foods per week (including spices, herbs, nuts, seeds). This correlates most strongly with microbiome diversity.

items.title: Postbiotics and Short-Chain Fatty Acids (SCFAs)
items.text: Postbiotics are metabolites produced by bacteria during fermentation. The main ones are short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs): acetate, propionate, and butyrate. Butyrate is the main fuel for colonocytes (intestinal cells), it strengthens the intestinal barrier, reduces inflammation, regulates apoptosis (programmed cell death) of cancer cells, and enters the bloodstream, exerting systemic anti-inflammatory effects, including in the brain. Propionate is involved in appetite regulation and gluconeogenesis in the liver. Acetate is a substrate for cholesterol synthesis. SCFA levels in stool are a direct marker of microbiome health and adequate fiber intake. Low butyrate is associated with 'leaky gut syndrome,' inflammatory bowel diseases, and colorectal cancer.
items.tip: Increase butyrate in two ways: 1) eat more soluble fiber (oats, legumes, root vegetables), 2) include products containing butyric acid directly: butter (especially from grass-fed cows), hard cheeses (Parmesan).

quiz.q: Where is 90% of the body's serotonin produced?
a: In the enterochromaffin cells of the gut
Indigestible fibers that serve as food for beneficial bacteria

quiz.q: What percentage of the immune system is associated with the gut?
Which bacterium is associated with leanness and healthy metabolism and feeds on polyphenols?
Which short-chain fatty acid is the main fuel for intestinal epithelial cells?
